data.title: From Alternative To Essential: The Expanding Role Of Private Market Investing
data.description:
Not long ago, private markets sat on the fringes of most portfolios – niche strategies used to pick up extra yield around the edges of traditional portfolios. That’s no longer the case. Over the past two decades, private markets have moved steadily into the mainstream, propelled by strong performance relative to public markets, a broader opportunity set, and greater accessibility for investors of all sizes. What was once considered an alternative is now becoming essential.

This evolution isn’t just about performance; it is also about relevance. The investment landscape is shifting under the weight of powerful forces: aging demographics, rapid digitization, and the global drive to decarbonize. Each of these trends is creating new capital needs and, in turn, new opportunities for private lenders and investors willing to take a longer-term view.

With more than 60 years of experience investing in private markets, Principal Asset Management has observed how quickly the definition of “core” continues to evolve. The expanding opportunity set across private real estate, infrastructure debt, investment-grade private credit, and direct lending underscores a growing need for more flexible, durable sources of return. As investors seek steadier income and growth in a shifting market environment, private markets are increasingly positioned at the center of long-term portfolio construction.

Private Real Estate

From reset to reinvention

The commercial real estate (CRE) market has undergone a significant reset and now appears positioned for renewed growth. Valuations have declined, in aggregate, by roughly 20–25% over the past two years, leaving sentiment cautious but improving. Historical patterns suggest this cycle is following a familiar trajectory: public markets have already stabilized, and private valuations are beginning to recover, with several CRE segments posting consecutive quarters of positive returns.

As the market resets, investors are shifting back toward fundamentals, focusing on property-level cash flows, balance sheet discipline, and regional supply-demand dynamics. The perception of CRE as primarily office, retail, and multifamily assets no longer fits today’s landscape. Instead, CRE has evolved into a $25 trillion ecosystem spanning 18 subsectors, many of which are linked to long-term demographic and technological shifts.

Changing composition and structural growth drivers

The modern CRE market increasingly reflects the needs of a changing economy, with new sectors emerging as key sources of growth. Data centers and senior housing have become significant components of the investable universe, benefiting from both demographic and technological forces. The rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) is intensifying demand for power infrastructure and purpose-built data facilities, while an aging population is driving sustained investment in senior and assisted living communities. Together, these trends are reshaping CRE strategies, pushing investors toward assets that align with digital transformation, the energy transition, and evolving societal needs rather than traditional property types alone. As a point of reference, nearly 70% of listed REIT market cap is now concentrated in “next generation” property types compared to ~40% for the private real estate market.

Subsector insights and emerging themes

Rental housing: Headlines often cite a housing shortage of millions of units, and while the underproduction is real, the framing is incomplete. The more pressing challenge is a mismatch between the type and location of housing and where demand actually exists. Rentership remains a cornerstone of the housing market, accounting for approximately 35% of households. But apartments are just one part of the story. Single-family rentals, manufactured housing, seniors housing, and even student housing are all meaningful and growing segments of the rental landscape. A holistic investment approach that spans geographies and the full spectrum of rental housing is needed in this market.

Office market: The post-pandemic correction in the office sector remains pronounced. Average-quality properties across the top 50 markets have seen valuations fall more than 60% from their peaks, while higher-quality properties owned by listed REITs are down 36%. However, according to JLL, roughly 90% of U.S. office vacancies are concentrated in just 30% of properties, and largely in older, less competitive buildings. In contrast, modern, energy-efficient buildings in prime locations continue to attract tenants and remain relatively resilient. Indeed, while private market office valuations for average-quality buildings across the top 50 markets sit at their lows overall, 19 of these markets are now seeing valuations rise. U.S. public REITs, which are often a leading indicator, have already rebounded 60% from their October 2023 lows. As capital re-enters the sector, selective lending or even opportunistic acquisitions of high-quality, well-located assets may present compelling value in the private real estate space.

Data centers: Data centers sit at the intersection of real estate and infrastructure—and increasingly form part of “digital real assets” strategies that span both domains. Why might data centers be viewed as infrastructure? They share many defining traits with traditional infrastructure assets such as toll roads, utilities, and airports:

  • Essential service function
  • Long-term, contracted revenue streams
  • High barriers to entry
  • Structural demand growth
  • Leases are tied to power availability, instead of the size of the usage area

These features support stable, long-term cash flows, relative insulation from economic cycles, and strong secular growth drivers, chiefly cloud adoption, AI adoption, and the exponential rise in digital data. However, despite their strong historical performance in both public and private markets, data centers remain underrepresented in many institutional real estate portfolios.

Positioning and portfolio implications

While some investors remain cautious, CRE has already absorbed its repricing and is now entering a more constructive phase, with valuations rising to varying degrees. The asset class has become less cyclical with the emergence of alternative property types, like data centers, with secular growth drivers. However, understanding the interaction between public and private markets, as well as debt and equity, remains essential. For many institutional portfolios, CRE’s hybrid characteristics, combining income-like stability with equity-like upside, make it a compelling diversifier.

As valuations start to recover after a 2-year correction and new growth areas emerge, investors who focus on strong fundamentals and disciplined selection are likely to uncover opportunities that combine steady income, moderate growth potential, and durable long-term value.

The takeaway

Commercial real estate is emerging from a broad valuation reset with more attractive pricing, stronger underwriting discipline, and a wider range of investable opportunities. While challenges persist in specific sectors such as office, the broader market’s structural evolution, toward data, housing, and social infrastructure, supports a more constructive outlook. For investors, CRE offers renewed relevance as a source of differentiated yield and portfolio diversification amid shifting macro and capital market conditions.

Private Infrastructure Debt

Stable and predictable cash flows—resilient, essential hard assets

Few asset classes embody durability as clearly as private infrastructure. Its long record of performance—through the dot-com bust, the Global Financial Crisis, commodity shock events, and even the pandemic’s severe disruption to global transportation—underscores its resilience. In each downturn, the asset class has not only recovered but also evolved to meet new economic realities. That strength is rooted in the essential nature of the underlying assets: ports, airports, roads, rail, utilities, power, energy, digital networks, and other social assets that underpin both local and global economies. The contractual and regulated nature of these assets contributes to consistently low default rates and stable income streams, a combination that long-duration investors find especially compelling.

A diverse and evolving opportunity set

Principal’s private infrastructure strategy comprises five broad categories: power, energy, transportation, digital, and social, each with distinct themes and growth drivers. Transportation often serves as the anchor exposure, complemented by strategic allocations to areas such as power generation, energy, digital, and social infrastructure.

Managers with expertise across both real estate and infrastructure are particularly well positioned to capture opportunities in social infrastructure, which includes healthcare, housing, and education, and in digital infrastructure, where data centers have matured into an asset class of their own. This cross-sector approach allows investors to balance stable, largely contractual cash flows with exposure to growth-oriented segments of the economy.

Secular tailwinds and long-term growth drivers

Looking forward, private infrastructure stands to benefit from powerful structural shifts already reshaping global markets:

  • The energy transition demands massive investment in power and grid modernization.
  • The growth of AI and digitalization is fueling demand for data centers, fiber networks, and reliable power sources.
  • Demographic shifts and aging populations are expanding investment needs in housing, including long-term care facilities and other social infrastructure.

The intersection between the energy transition and digital expansion is especially notable. As economies electrify and digitize simultaneously, new projects will emerge to reinforce aging power grids, expand power and transmission capacity, enhance grid reliability, and address water infrastructure needs—areas that continue to attract long-term capital.

The need for infrastructure investments is global. Europe, for example, continues to advance energy transition mandates with greater EV adoption and ongoing decarbonization efforts, creating investment potential in transportation electrification and power and energy.

Risk, liquidity, and the role of infrastructure debt

While the opportunity set is broad, investors must navigate familiar challenges, including potential for policy uncertainty and regulatory changes. Illiquidity also remains a key consideration from a portfolio construction lens. Private infrastructure benefits from a small but growing secondary ‘by appointment’ market, providing investors with some exit options.

Infrastructure debt, in particular, offers an attractive balance of risk and return. With stable and predictable cash flows, it provides defensive characteristics in uncertain environments and serves as a resilient portfolio through economic cycles. As a result, it can serve both a stabilizing role in diversified portfolios and a strategic role in meeting long-term income and liability objectives.

High yield infrastructure debt extends these advantages. It offers investors access to the same essential, often hard-asset sectors, but with enhanced yields to compensate for complexity or structural considerations. As such, it can serve as a complement to both investment-grade infrastructure debt and traditional high yield credit, delivering income with greater resilience for tailored portfolio solutions.

The takeaway

Private infrastructure continues to demonstrate its relevance as a long-duration, income-generating asset class with stable fundamentals and strong structural support. High yield infrastructure debt also provides complementary portfolio construction solutions to other high yield fixed income products. While policy uncertainty and illiquidity require careful management, the combination of predictable cash flows, hard assets and collateral, and yield premiums above public alternatives positions the asset class as a durable component in portfolios focused on capital preservation and steady income generation.

Investment-Grade Private Credit

Customized capital in a public-grade world

Investment-grade (IG) private credit occupies a unique space in private markets, offering investors the stability of high-quality borrowers with the structural advantages of privately negotiated terms. It’s a corner of the credit market that rarely makes headlines, precisely because it delivers what investors value most: steady performance and limited volatility. Yet even this relatively insulated segment has evolved, shaped by shifts in regulation, borrowing patterns, and the growing role of nonbank lenders.

A steady source of incremental return

For investors, IG private credit provides a way to deploy a portion of low-risk capital toward higher-yielding opportunities than those available in public fixed income. The asset class has become increasingly favored for its focus on security selection, strong structural protections, and relative resilience in volatile markets. Private placements, in particular, enable investors to capture yield premiums while maintaining exposure to investment-grade credits.

Illiquidity remains a key consideration. However, most transactions in this space carry ratings that meet institutional mandates, and the higher compensation for reduced liquidity often makes the trade-off worthwhile. The result is a segment that offers attractive income with less downside risk than comparably rated public bonds, especially in periods of widening credit spreads.

Structural strength and covenant protection

One of the defining features of IG private credit is its embedded covenant protection. These covenants help safeguard investors in the event of financial stress and allow them to take proactive measures long before a borrower defaults. Combined with the ability to sell positions in a modest but functioning secondary market, these features make private placements a reliable and flexible component of a defensive portfolio.

Emerging opportunities and challenges

Within the market, the asset-backed securities (ABS) segment offers a growing area of opportunity where investors can earn meaningful premiums, particularly in manager- or NAV-based lending structures. While the segment remains relatively young, its evolution could provide a valuable complement to traditional private placement strategies.

By contrast, renewable power projects will face headwinds due to shifting policy incentives and cost inflation, which will temper near-term deal flow. Meanwhile, new opportunities are emerging in structured finance linked to fiber-optic infrastructure, as population growth and digital demand drive investment in network expansion.

Companies looking to finance these projects off balance sheet increasingly turn to private markets, attracted by both competitive pricing and more flexible execution than in public bond markets.

The takeaway

For decades, Investment-grade private credit has remained one of the most stable and quietly effective segments of the private markets. Its combination of high-quality borrowers, covenant discipline, and incremental yield potential continues to make it a compelling option for investors seeking dependable income with downside mitigation.

Direct Lending

The middle-market growth engine

Direct lending has undergone transformational growth over the past two decades, driven by middle-market companies and private equity sponsors seeking flexible, customized sources of capital. The result is a mature and expanding market that now spans first-lien, senior secured, mezzanine, and hybrid equity loans, a far evolution from the leveraged-buyout-dominated landscape of the 1970s and 1980s.

This expansion has brought both opportunity and complexity. Investors are drawn to direct lending for its income potential, diversification benefits, and liability-matching capabilities. Still, they must also recognize its structural risks, including periods of illiquidity and evolving credit quality. As the market deepens, disciplined underwriting and thoughtful portfolio construction have become key to realizing its potential benefits.

Healthy fundamentals and market dynamics

Heading into 2026, the core indicators for direct lending remain broadly constructive. Borrower demand continues to rise as middle-market companies, and the private equity firms that own many of them, favor private, relationship-based financing over public markets and traditional bank channels. The opportunity set is diversifying, with non-bank lenders gaining market share due to their ability to offer speed, flexibility, and low-amortizing structures that free up company cash flows for growth investment.

Deal flow now comes through both private-equity sponsor and non-sponsor channels. Within the non-sponsor segment, one significant driver is the ongoing transfer of ownership among U.S. Baby Boomers, many of whom are selling or transitioning family-owned businesses. These succession-related transactions have become a consistent source of new lending activity and a long-term structural tailwind for the asset class.

Sector focus and emerging themes

Companies that tend to do well in this market are those that are growing, generating stable cash flows, and operate in industries that are not overly sensitive to swings in sentiment or the economy. Durable, essential businesses in sectors such as healthcare, accounting and legal services, as well as maintenance-based businesses across HVAC, plumbing, electric, auto repair, landscaping, and many others, continue to attract new capital.

At the same time, lending opportunities continue to expand across the software and technology sector as middle-market companies deliver products and services that are essential to their customers. New opportunities are emerging across technology-linked sectors as consumers and businesses increasingly invest in automation, IoT, and AI tools. In parallel, capital is flowing toward businesses that enhance the efficiency of aging utilities, power grids, and the data center ecosystem, highlighting the convergence of industrial and digital infrastructure themes that underscore direct lending’s expanding reach.

Positioning and portfolio implications

Middle-market companies account for roughly 44% of U.S. GDP, and their growing preference for private credit over syndicated or public high yield financing continues to fuel structural growth. For investors, direct lending offers a premium yield with greater structural protections, consistent cash flow, floating-rate income, and relatively low correlation to public credit markets, making it a valuable complement within diversified portfolios.

The trade-off, as always, is liquidity. While the asset class can perform well across market cycles, investors must be prepared to commit long-term capital and manage through periods of limited exit options. When executed with strong credit discipline, however, direct lending can enhance portfolio yield, provide downside mitigation, and serve as a stable, income-oriented allocation through changing market environments.

The takeaway

Direct lending has evolved into a core component of the private credit landscape, supported by structural demand from private equity sponsors and middle-market borrowers, which continue to migrate away from bank financing. Healthy fundamentals, diversification across industries, and the potential for attractive risk-adjusted returns underpin its institutional appeal. For investors able to accept its illiquidity, direct lending offers a compelling way to generate income and capture private-market growth in an increasingly yield-constrained world.

The case for private market investing

Investing in private real estate, private infrastructure debt, investment-grade private credit, and direct lending can enhance portfolio diversification and resilience, with each asset class contributing distinct strengths.

  • Commercial real estate offers attractive income potential and downside mitigation, benefits from secular demand tied to nonbank financing, and continues to deliver uncorrelated returns relative to equities and traditional fixed income.
  • Infrastructure debt provides long-duration, inflation-linked cash flows from essential assets, helping anchor portfolios during periods of volatility.
  • Investment-grade private credit brings the potential for incremental yield, customized structures, and covenant protections that are largely unavailable in public markets.
  • Direct lending targets the middle-market economy, capturing higher returns while providing structural safeguards and access to sectors less sensitive to macroeconomic swings.

These segments also complement one another. Real estate and infrastructure debt can play a defensive role, generating stable income and long-term value. At the same time, investment-grade private credit and direct lending can offer more opportunistic yield and growth exposure. Together, they work to reduce portfolio volatility, broaden diversification, and capture the illiquidity and complexity premia available in private markets.

The result is more balanced, forward-looking portfolios, designed not only to endure structural shifts but also to benefit from them. In an environment defined by higher capital costs, slower growth, and persistent macro uncertainty, the case for private market exposure has become strategic, not optional. Private markets are no longer a peripheral allocation; they are a central engine of durable, income-oriented performance across cycles.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Growth Mired By Margin Pressures
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) released fiscal Q1 earnings not long ago. While still guiding for high levels of growth, they have been plagued by margin pressures and what seems like a positional weakness in the AI opportunity. For that reason, I'm keeping my Hold rating.

Summary of Previous Thesis

I initially covered SMCI last year in the wake of its delayed SEC filings and short reports against it, rating it a Hold amid the uncertain financials and lack of steady free cash flow.

Back in June, I felt the stock was fairly valued, with updated financials and significant revenue growth to support a market cap near $26B and a share price around $42.

I was confident in some ways because of their 31% share of the branded AI server market, but I also took into account pressure on gross margins from competition and tariffs, with management guiding a lower range of 10% at the time. Lacking clear indicators of undervaluation, I rated the stock a Hold.

Recent Financials

SMCI ended fiscal 2025 with significant growth on the top line, but this was mired by the tighter margins I mentioned earlier.

Revenue grew 46.5% between 2024 and 2025, but gross margin declined from 13.8% to 11% in the same period. Their latest Form 10K explained the rise in cost of sales as such:

The year-over-year increase in cost of sales was primarily attributed to an increase of $6,353.2 million or 50.6% in costs of components, materials, and contract manufacturing expenses primarily due to increases in shipments of GPU servers, HPC, and rack-scale solutions which have higher costs and a $86.5 million or 493.6% increase in tariff expense related to new trade policies enacted during the year...

This was, nevertheless, the most cash-generative year in their history, with over $1.5B of free cash flow.

The revenue growth is almost entirely due to growth in their server products, driven up by demand for AI and the data centers to support them.

With a variety of hardware products, they've had plenty to offer in the AI opportunity. Nevertheless, Q1 results that came out this month showed that they are not on a straight line of growth.

Revenue was narrowly $5B, down 15% YoY and 13% sequentially. During the earnings call, management attributed this to about $1.5B of revenue that will be counted in Q2 instead. Gross margin also declined to 9.5%. FY 2026 revenue was still guided for $36B in the face of strong demand, reflecting additional growth.

Operating cash flows turned negative, down sequentially and YoY. As in previous quarters, even years, this largely stems from fluctuations in working capital, which are often significant for SMCI as they accumulate inventory to meet this demand. This gives them very lumpy free cash flow.

The balance sheet reflected this, with accounts receivable and inventories accounting for a majority of the assets, reflecting the significant investment in new products and why operating cash flows fluctuate between being positive and negative.

With most of their debt being the $4.7B convertible issues (due 2028 - 2030), largely backed by a $4.2B balance of cash, it's a healthy balance sheet, not over-leveraged in pursuit of growth.

Outlook and Valuation

The natural question at this point, and after so many quarters, is how much gross margin will take a hit in pursuit of this production. After all, free cash flow will not actually grow much if the gains on the top line are eaten away from the bottom. During the earnings call, they made their case for why gross margins should improve over time:

We do expect to benefit from some economies of scale driven by higher revenue levels, a cost-effective global manufacturing footprint, including our Malaysia facility and continued customer diversification. As we complete this mega cluster, we expect to leverage these investments and are establishing the most advanced AI service capabilities in the market. As we go through this transition, we expect our gross margins to improve.

Nevertheless, the issue was pressed heavily during Q&A by multiple analysts, and management conceded that it's a very long-term outlook for gross margins to return to double digits reliably. I think it begs the question of why strong demand does not give them more ability to price higher, and maybe it's because they are too low on the rung of AI to do that.

I think there's room for reasonable doubt that gross margins will rebound, and the valuation should be updated to reflect it.

The key changes from my last Discounted Cash Flow model are:

  • Baseline FCF raised to $700M.
  • CAGR for the latter half of the decade reduced to 10%.
  • Terminal multiple reduced to 15.

The FCF was increased, given the growth of their current assets and the kind of churn they should be able to get as they monetize those. Nevertheless, their struggles with gross margins and potential softening of demand after years of AI capex are spent and done make lower growth estimates, reflected in the later CAGR and terminal multiple, seem much more reasonable at this point.

This gives us an intrinsic value of about $21.5B, for about $32 per share. This suggests that the stock is about fairly valued and should average positive returns over time, but it does not present a significant discount, which I think is warranted for shareholders with the margin disappointments following the reporting issues. SMCI is just a hard stock to love at a fair value.

Conclusion

SMCI isn't a terrible company, but it's hard not to look at their growth and demand and wonder why their gross margins must take such a hit. Capital-intensive as they are, it doesn't seem easy for them to wriggle into a better position easily or quickly. Improvements may take years, and I think shareholders could get in at a better price if that's the case. That's why, despite the lower price, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund: 4 Reasons Why PDI Is Better Than PDO And PTY
data.description:
Investment thesis

In this paper, I would like to analyze four reasons why I recommend PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) as a “Buy.” The reasoning will be based on a comparative analysis with PDI’s main competitors, namely its “siblings” in the PIMCO fund family. The assets selected include PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY). Although, in addition to internal competitors, PDI also competes with external closed-end funds (CEFs), whose characteristic is the similar use of leverage to maximize the return on the asset portfolio. Major competitors of this type include the DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund (DSL), Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF), and BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT).

Nowadays, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is getting more popular because investors think it'll soon start growing for a long time. Starting with COVID-19 (February 2020), its security price has not recovered and has shown a record decline from $33.39 to $15.75 (October 23, 2025). In spite of the continuing downward trend, I think PDI has growth potential due to its interesting asset structure, portfolio formation history, and asset management scale effect (AUM $7.13 billion). It is important to remember the yield on payments, which stands at 15.05% forward yield.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund and its competitors in the PIMCO family

So, what is PDI and how does it differ from other funds in the PIMCO family? PDI is a closed-end fund (CEF) that operates in the multi-sector debt market. The primary assets in its portfolio are mortgage-backed securities (MBS), derivatives, and high-yield bonds. The fund typically invests at least 25% of its assets in private mortgage securities and may also invest up to 40% of its assets in securities of issuers economically related to emerging market countries. PDI's portfolio structure can be analyzed in more detail by looking at the composition of its holdings, paying attention to Sector Allocation (% Market Value). In total, the fund's portfolio contains 2,075 holdings.

Besides PDI, there are several other funds in the PIMCO family that are direct competitors. It is the other PIMCO funds that are often the most competitive rivals for PDI, as some can trade at a lower premium to net asset value, and some can offer more stable returns over a long period of time. Some of the most popular funds in the PIMCO family are PDO and PTY.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) is a young fund that is PDI's main domestic competitor, but with less history. PDO's investment strategy is highly similar, as it is focused on securities in the multi-sector debt market. What sets PDO apart is that it often trades at a lower premium than PDI, often making this fund more attractive. But while PDI has been around since May 2012, PDO only started in January 2021. That said, PDO's forward yield is lower (11.21%). Also, PDO has a smaller portfolio AUM of $1.827 billion.

PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) is one of the oldest funds in the PIMCO family (established in December 2002), which retrospectively shows a higher level of return than PDI. Its disadvantage, however, is primarily the high premium to NAV, which has historically ranged from 20% to 30%. Additionally, PTY's forward yield is 11.03%, which is less than that of PDI and PDO. To date, this fund manages assets worth $2.426 billion. Therefore, PDO and PTY together have AUM of $4.253 billion, representing 40.35% less than the AUM of the PDI portfolio. The difference reflects the growing popularity of the latter fund among investors seeking stable assets with high returns paid in monthly installments.

Comparative table of PIMCO fund characteristics

The following is a brief comparative table based on four key criteria, to help you see how PDI compares to its PIMCO family competitors, PDO and PTY.

Criterion

PDI

PDO

PTY

Asset type

High-yield MBS purchased at bargain prices

Assets issued after 2020

Balance between fresh corporate bonds and MBS

Premium

Premium of 5% to 15%

Permanent discount

High premium of 20% to 30%

Yield

Very high

Moderately high

High Portfolio focus

Portfolio focus

Mortgage securities market

Multi-sector

Corporate debt + High Yield

Advantages of PDI over its “brothers” PDO and PTY

In identifying the reasons why PDI is worth buying, I found four main factors that reflect the competitive advantages of this fund over PDO and PTY.

Reason #1. The structure of assets in the fund’s portfolio.

If you try to describe all 2,075 holdings in the PDI portfolio in one word, you might use the term “vintage.” The thing is, the PDI portfolio was formed mainly through the acquisition of another existing fund in the PIMCO family, PCI. The absorbed portfolio had been formed over many years and included a huge number of private mortgage bonds that were purchased after the 2008 financial crisis. The buying price for non-agency MBS assets was super low, as many of them lost over 90-95% of their value. At this point, we can say that these outdated mortgage securities are still stable. By having a fund like PDO buy these assets at current market prices, the PDI has built up a “safety cushion” of cheaply bought assets. Therefore, the PDI can get a higher level of return relative to the amount of capital invested.

Reason #2. Large portfolio of assets under management.

As I mentioned in my comparative analysis with other PIMCO funds, PDI is characterized by its large AUM of $7.13 billion. This is over 40% more than the combined portfolios of PDO and PTY. As such, PDI's liquidity is significantly higher, attracting larger institutional investors (due to higher daily liquidity in the market). This is coupled with large purchases of this asset, which helps protect institutional investors from slippage in the order book. Besides, such a large AUM makes PDI a widely diversified fund, mitigating potential risks for generating income from the default of one or several issuers.

Reason #3. The “golden mean” of the premium to NAV.

While analyzing PDO and PTY, I have noticed that one of the funds has a favorable discount for purchasing assets, and the other, on the contrary, a high premium reflecting a markup that makes the investment less profitable. The PDI is a kind of “golden mean” between the premiums to NAV of other funds in the PIMCO family. The premium to NAV for PDO is often in the range of 5% to 15%. It can be analyzed using the yield chart for the last year, where you can see situations in the market when the premium provides a good opportunity to enter the asset. Right now, for example, that premium has dropped. The market price is $17.58, and the NAV price is $16.82. That premium is only 4.3%. Whereas on October 1, 2025, the values were $19.84 and $17.02, respectively. This gave a premium of 14.21%. Therefore, now is a good time to buy PDI with a low premium.

Reason #4. Complexity of the investment strategy.

There might be a misconception that there is no advantage to the complexity of the investment strategy, as PDI faces an additional management burden. But, it is precisely the uniqueness of the aggressive hedging and derivatives investment strategy that makes this fund more competitive. PDI is headed by Dan Ivascyn. He employs one of the most complex strategies on the CEF market, rarely used by other fund managers. The core of this strategy is that PDI aggressively uses leverage and interest rate swaps. In a period when interest rates are expected to change, the fund can generate excess returns. The excess returns are higher than those generated by conservative investment strategies involving hedging and the use of derivatives. Due to this approach, PDI is able to achieve a fairly high average annual total return (%) of 10.11% over a 10-year period.

Risks of the investment thesis

There are risks to investing in PDI, as with a bunch of other PIMCO funds. Because the fund has a high return (around 15% per year), it reflects some cost for the managers using a complex portfolio structure and aggressive leverage to get there. The most obvious risk is related to premium compression. But, since the current premium is pretty low (around 4%), that risk is lower than it was for those who bought PDI when the premium was up to 15% (like a month ago). There is also a risk from using aggressive leverage. If leverage is forcibly reduced, the market value of the fund's assets could decline significantly. The PDI will have to sell part of its portfolio at poor prices, causing losses to be recorded. Further, they will reduce the fund's return, which will affect the size of the potential next dividend payment.

Conclusion

In summary, the primary advantage of PDI, determining its high investment appeal, is its access to an investment portfolio of mortgage-backed securities that were purchased at bargain prices following the 2008 financial crisis. That said, this fund has huge liquidity (AUM of $7.13 billion), high yield (15.05% per annum), and a successful track record of using complex hedging and derivative strategies. If you're looking to get the highest returns and want to buy an asset that has a low current premium to NAV (around 4%), then PDI is one of the best choices available in the current US fund market.

data.title: BNY Mellon International Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Summary

Global equities rose during the third quarter, as easing trade tensions reduced the likelihood of an economic downturn and investors cheered an interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve (the “Fed”), along with the prospect of further reductions this year. Continued excitement about artificial intelligence (AI) and some favorable corporate results supported the rally, which helped many stock indices hit record highs around the world. Markets came under some pressure, as investors briefly fretted about high AI valuations and amid anxiety about the US administration’s attempt to fire a Fed policymaker, which raised worries about possible political interference. Some disappointing economic data in China and uncertainty about a possible US government shutdown also caused unease in September.

BNY Mellon International Equity Fund (Class A at NAV) produced a positive return and outperformed its benchmark index, the MSCI EAFE (“the index”), during the third quarter of 2025. Both stock selection and asset allocation were positive overall versus the benchmark. Stock selection within the IT and energy sectors was positive.

The global equity market remained in a risk-on environment from the previous quarter, which saw continued enthusiasm for technology, notably artificial intelligence-related stocks. The materials and communication services sectors also performed strongly, driven by higher prices in some commodity markets, and solid corporate results, respectively. Traditionally defensive sectors such as consumer staples and healthcare underperformed. Healthcare continued to be impacted by tariff and pricing concerns within the pharmaceutical industry.

Top Contributors

data.title: Bitcoin: Blood On The Streets, Buy Signals Flashing
data.description:
To say the very least, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has had a tumultuous two months. It topped in early October at about $126,000 and is now down at $82,000 at the time of writing. I must admit that I was caught rather flat-footed by this move, because I had expected a run-up to over $175,000 and an extended cycle that would turn somewhere in Q1 2026. This would be aligned with the 4-year cycle, albeit slightly extended in time frame. What seems to have happened is the 4-year cycle has still held up, but the top arrived earlier than expected.

In this missive, I want to contextualize where we are in regard to a few very consistent indicators that are good at predicting an upturn in BTC. I will highlight a handful of indicators, discuss what they are, and discuss what they are saying about the market.

Options/Volatility Surface Indicators

The main thing to grasp is that options markets tell us much of what we need to know with regard to times of extreme fear. I haven’t found them to be a great indication of exuberance, but fear is where it gets it right. This is likely due to people using options in certain discernible actions when instances of extreme fear arise. These behaviors show up in the implied volatility surface and can be spotted when compared against the state of the surface at other times.

The first options-based indicator is, of course, the VIX – a model free implied volatility measure, called model free because it does not use the Black-Scholes options pricing model to compute an implied volatility. VIX is computed using a strip of vanilla options. Like the SPX, BTC has a VIX indicator too. DVOL is the name of this index, and it is computed using Deribit options on BTC. Here is what DVOL looks like:

You can see that it has reached the levels in early April 2025 where BTC bottomed from the tariffs scare. However, it is nowhere near the peaks from Terra Luna in May 2022 nor from FTX in November 2022. Does it have to get there to find a bottom?

Now, another important fact to know about Bitcoin is that, as opposed to equities, it tends to exhibit positive spot-vol covariance, which means that as the spot price moves up, volatility can actually increase. This limits the usefulness of simple VIX as a singular indicator for fear.

The next one is skew: normally regarded as the difference in the implied volatilities of the 25 delta call and the 25 delta put. When puts are priced at a significant premium to calls (assuming both are the same delta), it indicates significantly more demand for protection. Here is the skew of BTC options, with many different maturities.

Skew has been trending up for months. The 3 month skew is actually the highest it's been since the Silicon Valley Bank incident in March 2023. The 1-month skew is where it was in April 2025. However, skew on all timeframes is far off from the FTX incident in November 2022, which marked the bottom of the last cycle.

Next up is the term structure of implied volatility. This is the difference in IV between the 1-month expiry and the 6-month expiry. In general, backwardation in the IV term structure, where near-term is higher than further term, indicates a high degree of fear. This is consistent in equity markets as well. You can see it in both the term structure of VIX futures (which measures the future price of the SPX 1-month IV) and in the term structure of SPX options at each tenor (which is the construct we have here).

The term structure is now in deep backwardation and quite close to the FTX bottom. It is the highest it's been all cycle. What is also remarkable is how smoothly it climbed since September as the BTC moved to a new all-time high and then crashed over 30% in the course of two months.

MRVR On-Chain Indicator

MRVR uses the concept of realized gains of coins based on the last time they moved on the blockchain. The idea is that cycle bottoms form when the aggregated coins across the entire supply are in the red. The consistency of this indicator is quite remarkable. As with options indicators, it’s not great at calling tops but it has a knack for finding the cycle bottoms.

Considering that MRVR is now at 1.47, it means that the supply right now is still up 47%.

At this point, there are a few things to note. The start of 2024 was also the start of the institutional era in Bitcoin. While it is true that institutions will ultimately do on-chain transactions at some point, it is also true that the way institutions do things means that a single UTXO does not have to move for an institutional transaction to happen. What I mean by this is that when BTC was a person-to-person network, a transaction was almost always on-chain. When institutions are dealing with BTC that are held by custodians, the on-chain movement of a UTXO is often unnecessary because the same custodian can hold the UTXO but just update their ledger with the new ownership. This is a fundamental change in the market structure of how BTC is used, and this change will likely affect where MRVR bottoms out.

Second, just because the aggregate supply of BTC is up 47% does not mean the market must fall by that much before finding a bottom. A simple way to see this is to consider if all the UTXOs suddenly moved. This would automatically reset the cost basis of the entire supply to the current market price, since MRVR starts counting a coin as “new” right after it moves. If this were to happen, then no sales of BTC would need to happen (thus price cannot change), but MRVR would immediately go to 1 because the current supply would have a cost basis equal to the current market price.

Third, it is very likely the market finds a floor well before MRVR falls below 1. Considering that the pattern has persisted for four cycles, it is likely that traders will front-run the event and cause the bottom to form much earlier than normal. Ever since the launch of the spot ETFs, MRVR has found support around 1.7–1.9. And unlike prior cycles, the volatility of MRVR has been much more muted, along with the volatility of BTC.

Verdict

I’m assigning a Strong Buy rating to BTC based on the market internals due to the risk-reward presented by the market today. I believe it is very important to understand that the systemic-level risks from FTX, Celsius, Voyager, and 3AC in the last cycle are simply not present this cycle. There isn’t the same kind of opaque leverage in the system. In truth, the only bad news around BTC has been that long term holders have been selling, but even this should be expected if you consider that half of all BTC that will ever exist was released in the first four years and could have been mined using personal computers at effectively zero cost basis. Those early adopters are at the stage of rotating out of BTC and into enjoying life.

The 4-year cycle will likely break soon. It arguably already has, given that some things happened earlier than expected. For example, the price hit a new ATH before the Halving in April 2024. It also entered into months of consolidation after the Halving, which was very unusual. Traditionally the bear markets that end the cycle have lasted a year with over 70% drawdowns, BTC is now much more mature with an institutional audience that, while not prone to panicking, is prone to rebalancing. This kind of behavior is very likely to create a floor before a more dramatic downturn.

The ultimate backdrop of all of this is a U.S. government that is entering midterms with a record amount of issues, which can only be solved by injecting liquidity into the system. After Powell exits, the new Fed Chair will likely be much more accommodative to President Trump, who has been very adamant about lower interest rates and sending markets, including crypto, much higher.

Risk

The main risk to this thesis is that it plays out exactly like the traditional 4-year cycle. This could have us see a bear market that extends to the summer of 2026. BTC is already down 30%. Assuming a 60% drawdown from top to bottom would put the bottom at ~$50,000. That is a 40% drop from current levels.

As I’ve said before, I don’t see any real fundamental issues that could hurt BTC. The macro and the market internals all signal that a reversal is near. The only real risk, therefore—in my opinion—would be the market getting ahead of the 4-year cycle and turning it into a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Conclusion

While I was correct that the bear market in BTC would happen within 9 months of my last article, I was wrong in that I expected a larger rally with a blow-off top. What has transpired instead is a quick -30% crash. In the long term, I view this as a favorable turn of events that distributes coins to much stronger hands, while flushing out much of the fragile derivatives leverage.

Right now BTC looks very attractive based on the short-term indicators of fear, with the main risk being that the 4-year cycle continues.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management: Reality Check For High Growth Rates
data.description:
In July 2025, I published my last article about the Canadian alternative asset manager Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) and I argued that caution was the better choice at this point. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

I certainly won’t advise anyone to short Brookfield Asset Management, although I see another steep drop (30% to 50%) being very likely. We are dealing with a great business, but one trading for high valuation multiples and the risk of contracting valuation multiples should not be ignored. In combination with growth estimates by analysts and management maybe being too optimistic for the years to come, I would not buy the stock close to all-time highs. In my opinion, we will get the chance to buy the stock at least for its April 2025 lows again and probably even lower.

Up until now, we have not reached April 2025, but the stock declined 11% meanwhile and is currently trading 22% below its previous all-time high. Especially in the last few days, the sell-off has accelerated, and Brookfield Asset Management has lost more than 10% in value since last Wednesday.

In the following article I will argue once again that Brookfield Asset Management is rather a “hold” than a “buy”—despite the stock price being lower. I still ascribe a rather high probability to the scenario of lower growth rates in the years to come due to a potential recession and the AI spending boom coming to an end. And Brookfield Asset Management will be affected by this—despite reporting high growth rates and solid quarterly results up until now.

Quarterly Results

On November 7, 2025, Brookfield Asset Management reported third-quarter results for fiscal 2025. And while the company did beat slightly on earnings per share estimates, it missed revenue estimates by $90 billion (about 7% below expectations). Year-over-year fee revenues increased from $1,210 million in Q3 '24 to $1,390 million in Q3 '25 – resulting in 14.9% top-line growth. Fee-related earnings also increased 17.1% year-over-year from $644 million in Q3 '24 to $754 million in Q3 '25, while distributable earnings per share increased only 7.9% year-over-year from $0.38 in Q3 '24 to $0.41 in Q3 '25.

During the third quarter, Brookfield Asset Management deployed $23 billion in capital—a record quarter in deployment. The money was especially deployed in infrastructure ($9.3 billion during the quarter) and in credit ($9.9 billion). In the case of infrastructure, the money was put towards the acquisition of Hotwire Communication ($3.8 billion) and the acquisition of Colonial Enterprises ($3.4 billion).

While Brookfield Asset Management deployed $23 billion in capital, it raised about $30 billion during the third quarter (another record). Money was especially raised for Renewable Power & Transition ($6.3 billion) and Credit ($16.0 billion).

Year-over-year, fee-bearing capital increased 7.8% year-over-year from $539 billion in Q3 '24 to $581 billion in Q3 '25. This was especially due to inflows of $92 billion (partly offset by $22 billion in outflows and $26 billion in distributions). Credit still has the most fee-bearing capital—$263 - $263 billion and contributed 7.8% year-over-year growth. The two other drivers of growth were Real Estate (8.5% year-over-year growth) as well as Renewable Power & Transition (19.0% year-over-year growth).

Growth Rates

Analysts as well as management are remaining very optimistic that Brookfield Asset Management will continue to grow at a high pace. For the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 15.34% in these five years.

Management is similar or even more optimistic. During the last Investor Day in September 2025, the company looked back at the goal set for the last five years to double between 2020 and 2025 and the goal for the next five years until 2030 is to double again. Fee-bearing capital is expected to grow from $563 billion in September 2025 to $1.2 trillion in 2030 resulting in a CAGR of at least 16%.

During the last earnings call, CFO Hadley Peer Marshall commented:

These drivers should enable us to double our business over the next 5 years with fee-related earnings reaching $5.8 billion, distributable earnings reaching $5.9 billion, and fee-bearing capital reaching $1.2 trillion.

Management is seeing different drivers of growth for the years to come. Especially Credit is expected to drive growth, and fee-bearing assets are expected to increase from $254 billion in 2025 to $640 billion in 2030. The company will combine different strategies to reach these growth targets in the next five years—like the acquisition of the remaining 26% stake of Oaktree and Wealth Solutions growing at a high pace.

CEO Bruce Flatt commented on the path for the next five years:

Another important milestone was our recently announced agreement to acquire the remaining 26% in Oaktree Capital Management. As you know, one of the most respected names in global credit investing. When we partnered with Oaktree 6 years ago, the goal was to combine our global scale and real asset expertise with Oaktree's deep credit experience and value-oriented culture. That partnership exceeded expectations, enabling the rapid expansion of our credit platform, supporting the launch of Brookfield Wealth Solutions, and driving a 75% increase in Oaktree's asset base. Bringing Oaktree fully into Brookfield is the next natural step. It combines the scale and reach of our nearly $350 billion credit platform, enables deeper collaboration across our businesses from origination and underwriting to distribution and analytics. Most importantly, it enhances our ability to deliver the full breadth of Brookfield's credit capabilities to clients.

And finally, Brookfield Asset Management currently has $125 billion in uncalled commitments at this point, with $55 billion in funds not being fee-earning. This could lead to $550 million in additional fee revenue for the company—even without additional fundraising.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

So far, Brookfield Asset Management is still presenting itself as a business with high-growth potential and stable earnings. However, the stock is also trading for high valuation multiples. At the time of writing, BAM is trading for 30.4 times earnings, and although this is very close to the lowest P/E ratio Brookfield Asset Management was trading for in almost three years, a valuation multiple of 30 can only be justified by high-growth rates.

When looking at the price-free-cash-flow ratio, we see even higher multiples, as Brookfield Asset Management is currently trading for a P/FCF ratio of 38.6.

Aside from simple valuation multiples, we can also use a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock (or calculate what growth rates are necessary for the stock to be fairly valued). As always, we use a discount rate of 10% (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least) and calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which was 1,631 million). The basis for our calculation will be the distributable earnings of the last twelve months, which were $2,577 million.

If we are optimistic and assume Brookfield Asset Management can grow 15% annually for the next ten years (analysts only presented a growth target for the next five years, but I think management is optimistic high-growth rates will continue), followed by 6% growth until perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $74.48 for the stock, and Brookfield Asset Management is clearly undervalued.

In my opinion, this is too optimistic, and I would not bet on Brookfield Asset Management growing 15% annually. In a more pessimistic scenario, we assume “only” 10% growth for the next 10 years (which is still a high-growth rate) and get an intrinsic value of $52.43 making the stock only slightly undervalued at this point. There is also a reason for me being more skeptical than management and analysts—the AI boom and focus on credit.

How Predictable Are Mid-Teen Growth Rates?

This is probably the question it comes down to: How predictable are growth rates around 15% for the business, and how likely is it that Brookfield Asset Management will be able to keep up these high growth rates? And management is not only describing 87% of fee revenue as long-term or permanent (and therefore very predictable), it is assuming the share will grow to 92% in 2030.

And fees for Brookfield Asset Management might be very predictable, and the company can assume it will earn similar fees in 2026 as in 2025. However, Brookfield Asset Management needs high, double-digit growth to justify the current share price, and these growth rates are less predictable and stable in my opinion.

Problem: AI Boom

Management is very optimistic about artificial intelligence and how it could contribute to growth in the years to come:

The acceleration of AI and data digital infrastructure, the accelerating demand for electricity and the improving strength in the real estate markets, each of these themes plays directly to our strength as an owner, operator and investor in real assets and together, they are fueling multiyear growth across the business.

Management is also expecting the AI-related infrastructure market to grow at a high pace over the next decade:

We estimate that AI-related infrastructure investments will exceed $7 trillion over the next decade. Brookfield's unique position, owning and operating across the full energy and digital infrastructure value chain gives us a tremendous advantage in capturing this opportunity.

Although Brookfield Asset Management is only collecting fees and not affected as much by a bubble bursting, it is always a problem if spending for AI and infrastructure will slow down. Brookfield Asset Management might not be affected by huge losses or face the risk of bankruptcy (which will probably happen to some AI companies), but it will be affected by growth rates for its fees slowing down to a point where investors might not spend additional money on AI for several quarters or years, putting Brookfield Asset Management’s fundraising under pressure. And the business must grow at a high pace to be fairly valued.

Problem: Credit

Currently, the credit business is extremely lucrative and a driver of growth for Brookfield Asset Management. But, in case of an economic slowdown, the credit business is facing huge risks, in my opinion. Banks, asset management, credit, and lending—these are categories associated with high levels of risk in case of a recession or economic slowdown.

Of course, Brookfield Asset Management is facing limited risks as it doesn’t lend the money itself and is therefore collecting fees while third parties are the lenders and are therefore facing the highest credit and default risks. It is not enough for Brookfield Asset Management to keep its fee-bearing capital (and therefore fees) stable or grow in the single digits. The company must grow in the double digits to justify the stock price, and in case of a recession, I don’t see the company growing its fee at a high pace.

Risks Summarized

Not only is the current AI boom probably a bubble that might burst and lead to much lower growth rates in the next few quarters (or years), but credit as well as real estate might also see much lower growth rates in case of a recession. And as Brookfield Asset Management is depending on high-growth rates to justify the current stock price, a recession or economic slowdown can create a problem for the stock price and shareholders.

Conclusion

I still see the risk of declining stock prices in the coming months and quarters. And while I don’t have a crystal ball either (similar to every other investor), I think the stock will decline at least to about $40 again. If it will decline further seems very difficult to predict. On the one hand, we have a business growing at a high pace (around 15%) and strong hints that high growth might continue (so far it is raising capital and still has “dry powder,” and a recession – if it happens – might also be an opportunity to deploy capital again and find great investments).

On the other hand, a recession and slowdown in spending – especially in credit and AI—might pose a high risk for Brookfield Asset Management. Sentiment might also turn in such a scenario and lead to a declining stock price and the risk of a steep sell-off. And as investors, I would rather stay on the sidelines at this point.

data.title: TE Connectivity: Reset Is Real, But The Valuation Already Knows It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TE Connectivity's (TEL) Q4 '25 results confirmed virtually all the points that I had made in my previous thesis. The company is more diversified, stable, and being driven by secular demand (rather than by cyclical volumes) in increasing measure. The DDN segment continues to grow at a pace far exceeding expectations and is clearly and most durably representative of TEL's relevance in the area of accelerated compute. Energy is evolving into a multi-year utility infrastructure platform as opposed to being a tactical segment. Although softness in the sensor space continues to weigh on the transportation segment, this segment has proved to have a degree of stability in recent periods. The company's margins have stabilized above 20%; the company's free cash flow generation has been strong; however, the company's valuation, which in my opinion broadly factored in all the transformation and improvement. Therefore, I am reaffirming my Hold rating.

Topline Analysis & Outlook

TEL had $17.3bn revenues for FY25 (+9% on a reported basis and +6% on an organic basis). Q4'25 revenue grew to $4.75bn with (+17%/+11% (YoY)) on a reported and organic basis. Importantly, orders in Q4'25 represented a 22% (YoY) and 5% (QoQ) increase and provide a solid visibility for the company to generate another year of solid top-line growth in FY26. Management is guiding revenue in Q1'26 to ~$4.5bn (i.e., low double-digit organic growth).

The aspect of the portfolio that is most profitably transformative is Digital Data & Networking. The timelines looking at FY25 have demonstrated that the growth in TEL's accelerated compute business is not only real but accelerating faster than our most optimistic views. AI interconnect revenue, which included accelerated compute, approached nearly $900mn in FY25, and I expect that to accelerate in FY26 as the new GPU platforms propagate more widely into deployment and the customer base expands from just hyperscale to include cloud enterprise and edge workloads. The change here is architectural; the GPU racks are getting denser, hotter, and power hungry, and TE is providing the necessary high-speed copper, power distribution, and custom backplane solutions to follow along. These sockets are typically "sticky," fueled by long qualification cycles and high switching costs. I expect DDN will be the fastest growing segment of the company through FY26, with higher growth rates at the beginning, but tapering into the mid-teens or lower twenties range by FY27-FY28 as the installed base continues to grow with the increasing reliance on AI and accelerated compute workloads.

Energy delivered yet another strong quarter, with revenues increasing 83% and 24% on an organic basis, and continues to evidence what appears to be a multi-year growth curve based on grid hardening, undergrounding mandates, renewable interconnections, and the expanded portfolio resulting from the Richards acquisition. Unlike many previous cycles, much of the investment that is being made currently on the grids in North America and Europe is regulatory-based, multi-year in nature, and linked to reliability requirements. With Richards, TE is now involved in a much more comprehensive part of the medium voltage and distribution value chain. I expect Energy to show double-digit growth in FY26 and then settle into a high single-digit to low teens growth profile through FY28 as comparables normalize, but demand is structurally enhanced.

Automation & Connected Living also showed some voluntary re-normalization in Q4 with organic growth of 11%, telling us that we have cycled through much of the inventory digestion phase that challenged the business for much of FY25. Asia appears to be stabilizing, and the order flow looks to point to a healthier industrial backdrop into FY26. I expect ACL to continue to deliver sustained high single digits of growth in FY26 and begin to moderate gradually to mid-single digits of growth into FY27/FY28 as industrial PMIs stabilize and capex patterns return to more normal rhythms.

Aerospace, Defense & Marine: Achieved steady performance with 7% organic growth in the fourth quarter of fiscal year 25. Strength in the production of narrow-body aircraft and steady performance in defense activity contributed to the result. Although Aerospace, Defense & Marine is not a material growth driver, stability is the one segment that provides a predictable program, stability. I think AD&M will continue to have mid single-digit growth profiles for the next couple of years with ongoing fleet replacement cycles and strong Western defense budgets.

Transportation segment Q4 FY25 revenue was $2.41bn, +4% & +2% (organic). The geographical divide doesn't appear to be changing: Asia continues to balance the persistent softness in Europe and North America. An increase in the content of electrification, ADAS, and zonal architectures replaces a flattening of the global unit volumes, which signals a stabilization. Although the recovery is uneven, commercial transportation picked up a little when fleet replacement cycles began to pick up in some places. Sensors remain a headwind. I expect Transportation to have a low- to mid single-digit growth profile through FY28, and increasingly take on the role of the stabilizing base of the portfolio as opposed to the growth engine.

As a whole, TE is entering FY26 with a more balanced revenue mix and structurally stronger demand profile than at any time during the last decade. DDN and Energy represent the secular growth engines, ACL is returning to normalized levels of absorption, AD&M is showing program-driven stabilization, and Transportation is providing duration.

Margin Outlook

TE closed FY25 with a consolidated adjusted operating margin of 19.7% (+80bps YoY), and 19.9% (+130bps YoY) for Q4. To me, it is not just the number that is important, but more the consistency: TEL maintained a ~20% margin profile under very different end market conditions, and it did it without taking advantage of temporary cost actions. Mix-up, volume leverage, in the right places, disciplined execution across the portfolio came from the uplift. Management's Q1'26 guide, with the new non-GAAP treatment that excludes intangible amortization, indicates a stable starting point; however, even considering the company on a like-for-like basis, the exit rate going into FY26 is an indication of a structurally stronger operating model than the company carried heading into FY25.

Transportation Solutions had a Q4 adjusted operating margin of 19.4%, essentially flat year on year, and certainly closed FY25 at close to the ~20% band management had long indicated as the steady state level for this business. The pattern throughout the year was familiar: content growth was offsetting uneven regional production, pricing held up, and sensors were one recurring drag on blended profitability. The segment is not set up for major margin expansion from here, based on global auto build being expected to stay broadly flat, but what stands out is the stability. TE no longer needs volume growth to defend Transportation margins - electronics content across EV platforms, zonal architectures, and ADAS systems is now the burger. Through FY26-FY28, and looking right now, I view Transportation as one of these margin anchors, one that's stable, predictable, and guaranteed support and not unit support.

Industrial Solutions is the place where the structural shifts are more visible. The segment had a Q4 adjusted operating margin of 20.3% (+300 bps YoY), and the company's management has stated that the second half of FY25 was running at ~20% (full year FY margin at 19.1%). The broader argument is that Industrial is now trading at a consistent margin level that was simply unattainable just a few years ago. Most of the drivers are a product mix. Digital Data & Networking is the most accretive contributor, with AI interconnect programs scaling into higher contribution architectures with long qualification cycles and engineering-heavy solutions. Energy is increasingly coming into play as a durable contributor to margin rather than an opportunistic contributor; the early stages of the Richards integration cause the typical friction in terms of footprints, alignments, but the underpinning economics of the undergrounding, modernizing the grid, and grid renewable interconnection economics are margin supportive as volumes build. Automation & Connected Living is slowly recovering back to normal incremental absorption as the correction of the manufacturers' inventory fades away, and Aerospace & Defense & Marine continues offering the normal program economics to refine and smooth the consolidated industrial profile. The common theme is that the mix at Industrial is continuing to move towards businesses with a better underlying contribution structure - i.e., a more secure underlying margin base.

Stepping back, TE comes into FY26 with a margin foundation that is more bothersome to navigate and structurally supported than they've seen in any recent cycle. Transportation is a floor that we can count on, Industrial is the Mix that is driven Upside, and the portfolio is now driven to the segments that compound the earnings rather than oscillate with short-cycle Volatility. There will be quarter-to-quarter fluctuations related to the phasing of AI programs or utility projects, but the medium-term outlook is simple:

Financial

Peer Comparison And Valuation

TEL's current trading multiples are a broad reflection of structural improvements in its portfolio. On forward numbers, the stock is trading around FWD P/E of 21~22x & 14.8x of FWD EV/ EBITDA, which puts the company in the low end of the highest growth connectivity companies but above the companies that have more cyclicality exposure in industrial components. The overall peer group is clustering at ~23x on FWD P/E, and ~17x on EV/ EBITDA, implying that TEL is neither undervalued nor overvalued, but trading at a valuation commensurate with a company that is transitioning towards more durable, revenue streams of better mixes. The multiples implied in the valuation represent what appears to be a plausible path of mid- to high-single-digit organic growth and gradual improvement in operating margins, but do not leave allowance for a large valuation gap.
